findings.summary: Heavy scientific and web-framework packages are declared as mandatory core dependencies rather than optional extras.
evidence: pyproject.toml lines 35-90 list numpy, scipy, networkx, fastapi, uvicorn, pydantic, paramiko, aiohttp, fastavro, mako, cryptography, GitPython as core [project.dependencies] — all unconditional installs.
advanced_plotter.py lines 13-16 import matplotlib, numpy, pandas, seaborn unconditionally at module level with no try/except guard, despite these being heavy optional visualization deps.
plotter.py lines 10-11 similarly do bare `import matplotlib.pyplot as plt` and `import numpy as np` at module top-level.
findings.suggestion: Move matplotlib, numpy, pandas, seaborn, scipy, fastapi, uvicorn, paramiko, aiohttp, fastavro into [project.optional-dependencies] extras (e.g., 'visualization', 'api', 'remote'). Guard unconditional top-level imports with try/except ImportError and raise a clear RuntimeError with install instructions at call time.

findings.summary: Two separate health_checker.py files exist at system_discovery/core/ and system_discovery/health/, creating ambiguous ownership and likely diverged implementations.
evidence: system_discovery/core/health_checker.py defines class SystemHealthChecker importing from coding, data_visualization, logging_monitoring.

system_discovery/health/health_checker.py defines a different health checker importing from coding.execution.executor, coding.static_analysis, performance, security.digital, logistics.orchestration — a much wider and more opinionated dependency set.

The health/ variant imports logistics.orchestration.project.workflow_dag and security.digital, crossing deep into the Service layer from what should be a Foundation utility — a layer-direction violation.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single health_checker.py under system_discovery/health/; remove or redirect the core/ version. Audit whether the health/ variant's imports of logistics and security violate the Foundation→Core→Service dependency direction, and move those checks to a higher layer if so.

findings.summary: The module docstring in event_schema.py is placed after the import block, making it a dead string literal rather than an actual module docstring.
evidence: event_schema.py line 1 begins with `import json` — no leading module docstring.

Lines 11-16 contain a triple-quoted string after the imports: `"""\nEvent Schema for Codomyrmex Event System\n..."""` — this is a statement expression, not a module docstring. Python only recognises the first string literal as __doc__.
The file also imports `get_logger` at line 9 before the try/except block that re-uses it at line 20-22, creating a redundant guarded re-assignment of the same name.
findings.suggestion: Move the triple-quoted description to the very top of the file (before all imports) so it becomes the actual module __doc__. Remove the redundant try/except logger re-assignment at lines 19-22 since get_logger was already imported unconditionally at line 9.

findings.summary: data_visualization/engines/plotter.py uses a try/except fallback to stdlib logging instead of unconditionally calling get_logger, unlike all peer modules.
evidence: plotter.py lines 24-34: `try: from codomyrmex.logging_monitoring.core.logger_config import get_logger; logger = get_logger(__name__) except ImportError: logger = logging.getLogger(__name__); if not logger.hasHandlers(): logging.basicConfig(...)`
advanced_plotter.py lines 21-23 uses the same try/except pattern but without the basicConfig fallback — inconsistent even within the same directory.
All other reviewed modules (calendar_integration/mcp_tools.py, trust_gateway.py, agents/pai/mcp/server.py) import get_logger directly with no fallback.
findings.suggestion: Remove the try/except guard; import get_logger unconditionally. logging_monitoring is a Foundation-layer module that all other modules may depend on — it should never be absent. The fallback path adds dead complexity.

findings.summary: Module-level __getattr__ caches computed values back into globals() — an unusual pattern that conflicts with the module's own use of _get_safe_tools() elsewhere.
evidence: trust_gateway.py line 296-310: `__getattr__` computes SAFE_TOOLS/SAFE_TOOL_COUNT/DESTRUCTIVE_TOOL_COUNT lazily and caches via `globals()[name] = val`. This means the value is fixed after first access, even if the tool registry changes.

Line 303: `SAFE_TOOL_COUNT` computation reuses `globals().get('SAFE_TOOLS') or _get_safe_tools()` — the `or` operator silently falls through if SAFE_TOOLS is an empty frozenset (falsy), computing it twice.

__all__ at line 804 lists SAFE_TOOLS/SAFE_TOOL_COUNT/DESTRUCTIVE_TOOL_COUNT with `# noqa: F822` comments acknowledging they are not actually defined at module scope — a smell that the API surface is fragile.
findings.suggestion: Replace the __getattr__ lazy-caching with simple module-level assignments after _get_safe_tools() is available, or expose them as functions (get_safe_tools(), safe_tool_count()) which are idiomatic and don't involve globals() mutation. Fix the falsy-frozenset bug in SAFE_TOOL_COUNT computation.

findings.summary: plotter.py imports numpy at module level but never uses it, wasting import time and misleading readers about dependencies.
evidence: plotter.py line 11: `import numpy as np` — np is not referenced anywhere in the first 70 lines of the file.
The Plotter class (lines 41-70) delegates entirely to chart functions and makes no direct numpy calls.
findings.suggestion: Remove the unused `import numpy as np` from plotter.py. If numpy is needed by a specific method, import it lazily inside that method.

findings.summary: git_check_availability() returns success unconditionally with no try/except, unlike all sibling tools in the same file.
evidence: mcp_tools.py lines 24-27: git_check_availability() calls _git().check_git_availability() with no try/except block — any exception propagates as an unhandled Python exception rather than returning {"status": "error", "message": ...}.
All sibling tools (git_is_repo, git_repo_status, git_current_branch, git_diff at lines 34-80) wrap their calls in try/except and return the standard error dict.
The calendar_integration, search, and coding mcp_tools.py all use consistent try/except wrapping for every tool function.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the body of git_check_availability() in a try/except block matching the pattern used by its siblings: `try: available = _git().check_git_availability(); return {"status": "success", ...} except Exception as exc: return {"status": "error", "message": str(exc)}`.

findings.summary: system_discovery has both a core/ and a health/ subpackage each containing a health_checker.py, creating structural confusion about which is canonical.
evidence: Glob of system_discovery/ shows: core/__init__.py, core/health_checker.py, health/__init__.py, health/health_checker.py, health/health_reporter.py — two separate subpackages both claiming health-check responsibility.
The presence of both a health_reporter.py in health/ and the SystemHealthChecker in core/ suggests these were designed independently and not integrated.
The core/ variant's imports (coding, data_visualization) differ entirely from health/'s imports (performance, security, logistics), implying no refactoring was done to unify them.
findings.suggestion: Designate health/ as the canonical health-checking subpackage. Move any unique logic from core/health_checker.py into health/ and delete the core/ file. Update __init__.py exports accordingly.

findings.summary: server.py creates closures for resource providers inside a for-loop without capturing the loop variable, risking late-binding bugs.
evidence: server.py lines 110-120: the for-loop `for uri, res_name, res_desc, mime in RESOURCE_DEFINITIONS:` defines nested functions `_modules_provider` and `_status_provider` with `def` inside `if/elif` branches — but the provider variable is reassigned in the same scope each iteration.

The pattern `provider = _modules_provider` assigns a newly-defined function, but if a future resource definition falls through to the else branch (line 120+), the loop variable `uri` captured by the closure would refer to the last iteration's value (classic Python for-loop closure trap).

The inline comment on line 97-98 `# The server uses its own internal registry, so we copy over / (or ideally server accepts a pre-built registry...)` openly acknowledges a design compromise left in production code.
findings.suggestion: Define resource provider factories outside the loop, or use functools.partial / default-argument capture (`def _provider(u=uri): ...`) to bind the loop variable at definition time. Remove the TODO comment from production code.
